flashcards.Back: The actual private IP address assigned to a host on the internal network. it is the source IP before any translation occurs.
The registered public IP address that represents one or more internal hosts to the outside world. it is the source IP after NAT translation.
The IP address assigned to a host on the external network (e.g., a server on the internet). it is the destination IP from the perspective of an internal host.
The IP address of an external host as it appears to the internal network. In most source NAT setups, this is identical to the outside global address.
A permanent one-to-one mapping between an inside local address and a specific inside global address. typically used for internal servers.
A translation method where internal local addresses are mapped to the first available public address from a pre-defined pool.
Also known as NAT Overloading. it allows multiple private IPs to be mapped to a single public IP by using unique source port numbers.
The specific Cisco configuration option that enables PAT, allowing thousands of internal hosts to share one or a few public IP addresses.
A range of registered public IP addresses defined on a router that can be used for dynamic NAT translations.
An Access Control List used in dynamic NAT or PAT configuration to identify which internal IP addresses are permitted to be translated.
The interface configuration command used to designate a port as being connected to the internal, private network.
The interface configuration command used to designate a port as being connected to the external, public network.
The most common form of NAT where the source IP address of a packet is translated as it exits the network toward the internet.
An internal database on the router that tracks every active NAT mapping, including the IP addresses and port numbers for both ends of a flow.
A statistic in 'show ip nat statistics' indicating the number of packets that were successfully matched against an existing translation entry.
A statistic indicating the number of times a packet required a translation but no matching entry was found in the table, potentially triggering a new mapping.
The duration of inactivity after which a dynamic entry is automatically removed from the NAT translation table to free up global addresses.
The standard that defines the private IP address ranges (10.x, 172.16.x, 192.168.x) that NAT is typically used to translate.
A PAT configuration where the router uses the IP address currently assigned to its outside physical interface as the global address for all internal translations.
The primary verification command used to view the list of all current NAT mappings in the router's memory.
A verification command that provides high-level data on NAT operation, including the number of active translations, pool usage, hits, and misses.
The command used to flush all dynamic mappings from the NAT table, useful for troubleshooting or after a configuration change.
A diagnostic command that provides real-time information about each packet being translated by the router.
A condition occurring when a dynamic NAT pool is completely exhausted, preventing any new internal hosts from accessing the internet.
A static mapping where a specific public port is directed to a private internal IP and port, allowing external access to a specific service behind the NAT.
A configuration (usually static) that allows both an internal host to initiate outward and an external host to initiate inward to that same internal host.
A scenario where two internal hosts communicate with each other using the public IP address of an internal server, requiring the router to translate and loop the traffic.
Large-scale NAT used by service providers to share a single public IP among thousands of customers, typically using the 100.64.0.0/10 address space.
The available pool of TCP/UDP ports (typically 1024–65535) that PAT uses to distinguish between different translated flows.
A configuration error where an internal private network uses the same IP range as an external public network, causing routing and translation failures.
